# Alamo Group (ALG) - A Deep Dive on Management and the Setup

*Eight consecutive earnings calls reviewed: the second quarter of 2024 (August 3, 2024) through the first quarter of 2026 (May 5, 2026). Two CEO eras (Jeff Leonard to Robert Hureau, with the handover completed in the third quarter of 2025). All figures cross-referenced against the screener's financial record.*

---

## 1. The story over time

Across these eight quarters, this is the story of a two-engine industrial running on one cylinder, told first by an outgoing CEO who insisted the second cylinder was about to fire, and then by a new CEO who reset the timeline and quietly admitted it would take years.

**Phase 1, Industrial offsets Vegetation, and Vegetation is bottoming (second quarter of 2024 through first quarter of 2025, Leonard).** The opening story was a clean divergence trade: the Industrial Equipment division (vacuum trucks, excavators, street sweepers, snow removal) was booming on post-COVID infrastructure money and fleet renewals, while the Vegetation Management division (forestry, tree care, agricultural and municipal mowing) was in a cyclical trough. Management's thesis: aggressively strip fixed costs out of Vegetation (close plants, cut global headcount by 14%, save $25-30M a year), keep Industrial humming, and emerge with structurally higher margins when Vegetation recovers. Leonard repeatedly dangled two future catalysts: a 15% operating margin target for both divisions, and a very active acquisition pipeline with a couple of big opportunities right in the company's sweet spot that would break loose in the first half of 2025 (third and fourth quarters of 2024, first quarter of 2025).

**Phase 2, Industrial is a record machine and Vegetation is recovering (first through second quarter of 2025, Leonard).** Through mid-2025 the bull story peaked. Industrial printed record quarterly sales ($227M in the first quarter of 2025, $241M in the second quarter of 2025) with operating margin expanding toward 14%, and Leonard claimed Vegetation had logged five straight quarters of improving orders (second quarter of 2025). The story was at its most confident here, and notably this is where Leonard announced his own succession was well advanced and would conclude in the third quarter (second quarter of 2025).

**Phase 3, the reset (third through fourth quarter of 2025, Hureau arrives).** The narrative turned hard with the new CEO. Robert Hureau's first two calls walked back the optimism: the third quarter of 2025 was mixed, gross margin slipped to 24.2% on unforeseen production inefficiencies from the very plant consolidations that were supposed to raise margins, plus tariff costs. Then the fourth quarter of 2025 was an outright air pocket: Vegetation profit margin collapsed to 2.3% (from 10.2% a year earlier), full-year adjusted earnings cratered to $1.70 a share from $2.39, and management took inventory-reserve charges on product lines it intended to divest or discontinue. Crucially, Hureau stretched the 15% margin goal from Leonard's end-of-2025 date to a vague through-the-cycle, two-to-four-year target hinged on a Vegetation end-market recovery.

**Phase 4, inflection underway (first quarter of 2026, Hureau).** The latest call is the rebound quarter: revenue up 6.7%, adjusted earnings of $2.56 a share (versus $1.70 the prior quarter), Vegetation profit margin back to 11.2%, and the Petersen Industries acquisition ($166.5M, about 7.9 times profit, closed January 2026) folded into Industrial. The new growth drivers introduced: a four-part plan (culture, commercial, operational, capital deployment), a company-wide procurement savings project, a parts-and-service mix initiative, and small bolt-on acquisitions (Ring-O-Matic plus Petersen) as the engine while organic Industrial demand admittedly cools off the infrastructure-stimulus peak.

**Drivers quietly dropped:** Leonard's couple of big acquisition opportunities breaking loose in the first half of 2025 never materialized as anything large; what actually closed were two small add-ons (Ring-O-Matic in mid-2025, Petersen in January 2026). Electrification research, hyped in 2024, was explicitly de-emphasized by the second quarter of 2025 (the drive and demand for that has taken a time out). The clean Vegetation is recovering order story of mid-2025 was overtaken by the fourth-quarter-2025 collapse, then reintroduced more cautiously in the first quarter of 2026.

---

## 2. Claims versus financials

The central tension: management's prose has been consistently more bullish than the actual results, and the gap is widening at the company-wide level even as the Industrial sub-segment genuinely delivers.

| Management claim | Quarter(s) | The actual financials | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "15% operating margin is very doable... within 2025, at least by end of year" | fourth quarter of 2024 (Leonard) | Full-year 2025 operating margin **fell**; the fourth quarter of 2025 was a 2.3% Vegetation-margin disaster. Target later pushed to two-to-four years, through the cycle, by Hureau (fourth quarter of 2025). | **Missed, and the goalposts moved.** The single clearest broken promise. |
| "Very active M&A pipeline... a couple of big opportunities... breaking loose in H1-2025" | third and fourth quarters of 2024, first quarter of 2025 (Leonard) | Actual deals were small add-ons: Ring-O-Matic (mid-2025) and Petersen ($166.5M, January 2026). Useful, but not the transformational deals implied. | **Over-promised.** Story richer than delivery. |
| "$25-30M annualized Vegetation cost savings; margins will expand" | third quarter of 2024 through first quarter of 2025 | Savings real, but third- and fourth-quarter-2025 gross margin *fell* on unforeseen production inefficiencies from the same consolidations; Vegetation full-year-2025 profit margin **2.3%** (fourth quarter) versus the implied structural uplift. | **Partly divergent.** The cost-cutting was real; the net margin benefit was swamped by lost volume leverage and execution slips. |
| Industrial "record" results, margin toward 15% | first through second quarter of 2025 | **Confirmed.** Industrial hit record sales ($240.7M in the second quarter of 2025), 17.7% full-year-2025 profit margin versus 15.7% prior. This is the genuinely strong leg. | **Matches.** The one claim the numbers fully support. |
| "Strong, durable balance sheet; net debt slashed" | every quarter | **Confirmed.** Net debt cut from $236M (June 2023) to about $11-23M through 2025; the screener confirms near net cash (live recompute: about $95M net debt *after* the $170M Petersen funding draw, still below one year of profit). | **Matches.** The balance sheet is the strongest objective fact in the file. |
| Company revenue resilient | second quarter of 2024 through first quarter of 2026 | Screener: revenue **down 1.5% year-over-year**, three-year compound growth rate of only **+1.9%**; the yearly path is $1.69B (2023) to $1.63B (2024) to $1.60B (2025), a multi-year *decline*. | **Story poorer than implied.** "Resilience" masks two years of top-line erosion. |
| Earnings power | - | Screener: earnings per share $11.36 (2023) to $9.63 (2024) to **$8.59 (2025)**, down 10.8% year-over-year; trailing-twelve-months diluted earnings **$8.36**. Free cash flow down 20.5% year-over-year. Beat its forecast only **3 of 7 quarters (43%)**. | **Deteriorating.** Peak-to-trough earnings down about 25% over two years; a sub-50% beat rate flags weak guidance discipline. |

The bottom line: the divisional story is honest (Industrial strong, Vegetation weak), but the company-wide story has been dressed up. "Resilient revenue" is actually a two-year decline, and the marquee margin promise was missed and then re-dated.

---

## 3. Tone and confidence over time

The trajectory was declining under Leonard, then reset to a new base and cautiously rising under Hureau. Net read: a confidence reset, not a collapse.

- **Second quarter of 2024 (Leonard):** Offense-defense blend. Very bullish on Industrial and on margin-expansion potential, but already managing the Vegetation problem with an urgent need to consolidate production facilities. *Confident.*
- **Third and fourth quarters of 2024 (Leonard):** Peak swagger about the future. A 15% operating margin in that segment is very doable within 2025 (fourth quarter of 2024); the 2025 acquisition pipeline is looking interesting, with a couple of big opportunities. *The most offensive language in the file.*
- **First quarter of 2025 (Leonard):** Still confident but increasingly hedged on Vegetation mix (I wouldn't factor in too much of a margin improvement... more like a fourth-quarter event).
- **Second quarter of 2025 (Leonard, final call):** Confident on Industrial records, but the succession admission lands (both a happy and a sad thing for me personally). Forestry caution creeps in: we've still seen some order cancellations in forestry... not completely out of the woods.
- **Third quarter of 2025 (Hureau, debut):** Deliberately reset. While I'm not pleased with the results, I am optimistic, the classic incoming-CEO clearing-the-decks framing. Margin-target language gets vaguer.
- **Fourth quarter of 2025 (Hureau):** Defensive on the print (2.3% Vegetation margin, $1.70 earnings) but forward-confident: more confident and excited today... than when I joined. Heavy emphasis on the process (four pillars) over near-term numbers.
- **First quarter of 2026 (Hureau):** Genuinely more upbeat with evidence behind it: sequential earnings nearly doubled, Vegetation margin recovered. But laces in fresh macro caution: a slightly more cautious tone... the tone is changing slowly here over the last 30 days (post-Iran-war freight and fuel costs).

The clear shift: from Leonard's offensive growth and margin-target language to Hureau's defensive efficiency, procurement and cost-discipline language, exactly as the cycle turned against them. Hureau is more specific and more conservative with guidance than Leonard was, a credibility upgrade in style even as the numbers were worse.

---

## 4. Guidance credibility

Reliability is medium, with a clear split by era: Leonard low on margin and acquisition promises, Hureau too new to fully judge but tracking high so far.

- **Leonard's record is poor on the headline promise.** The 15%-by-end-of-2025 margin target (fourth quarter of 2024) and the big-acquisitions-breaking-loose-in-the-first-half-of-2025 claim (third quarter of 2024, first quarter of 2025) both failed. The screener's 43% beat rate (three of seven quarters) confirms a pattern of guidance that ran ahead of delivery; there is no lowballing here, if anything the opposite (over-optimism).
- **Hureau has, so far, under-promised and over-delivered.** He explicitly reset expectations downward in the third and fourth quarters of 2025 (we will not get all the way back to... 2025 margin), then beat consensus handily in the first quarter of 2026 ($2.56 versus about $2.20 expected). His fourth-quarter-2025 caution that Vegetation needed to get back to about 8% adjusted operating margin before the journey to 15% began was a credible, granular framing, and the first quarter of 2026 delivered Vegetation profit of 11.2%, ahead of that bar.
- **The reliability watch-item:** Hureau's 15% operating-margin, 18% profit-margin, 10%-plus-growth through-the-cycle targets are the same numbers Leonard missed, now wrapped in a longer, vaguer timeframe and explicitly hinged on a Vegetation end-market recovery that is not yet confirmed (first-quarter-2026 agricultural orders up double-digits, but municipal mowing still down double-digits, and management flagging a softening macro tone). The targets are credible as a direction, not yet as dated commitments.

---

## 5. Analyst pressure points

The analyst bench (chiefly Mig Dobre of Baird, Greg Burns of Sidoti, Chris Moore, plus D.A. Davidson) circled the same wounds for two years:

1. **Can Industrial margin actually reach and hold 15%?** Pushed every call. Leonard kept saying yes (very doable... by end of 2025). Dobre forced the issue in the third quarter of 2025: we used to talk about the exit run rate... 15% operating margin. That clearly seems to be off the table... are we really looking at 12-13%? And Hureau conceded we're in that ZIP code. The optimistic answer turned into bad news.
2. **Vegetation margin recovery timing.** Repeatedly probed; management repeatedly said next quarter, a fourth-quarter event, sequential improvement, and it kept slipping. Dobre in the second quarter of 2025 explicitly tried to pin down the consolidation-cost drag (at what point should we expect this drag to be done with?), and Leonard declined to quantify (I don't think we want to do that on this call). A deflection that preceded the fourth-quarter-2025 margin collapse.
3. **Sustainability of Industrial demand after stimulus.** Dobre's sharpest line of questioning (third quarter of 2025): infrastructure stimulus dollars have frankly been spent... where are we in the replacement cycles? Hureau partly conceded (that will slow) and pivoted to hydro-excavation and acquisitions as offsets. This is the bull case's central unaddressed risk: what happens to Industrial when the federal money fully rolls off.
4. **CEO succession.** Dobre tracked it for a year (we haven't heard anything new... how is the Board looking at this?, second quarter of 2025). Resolved with Hureau, but the timing, a leadership change at the exact moment results deteriorated, was a governance question analysts kept live.
5. **Acquisition pipeline (the big ones).** Burns and Shlisky repeatedly asked what the big opportunities were; management never delivered the transformational deal it teased, only small add-ons.

---

## 6. Warning signs and encouraging signs

**Warning signs**
- **The marquee margin promise was missed and re-dated** (15% by end of 2025 to two-to-four years through the cycle). Same numbers, longer rope (fourth quarter of 2024 to fourth quarter of 2025).
- **Unforeseen production inefficiencies from the cost-savings program itself:** the consolidations meant to lift margin instead dented gross margin in the third and fourth quarters of 2025. Execution risk on the core thesis (third quarter of 2025).
- **The fourth-quarter-2025 earnings air pocket:** Vegetation profit margin of 2.3%, full-year adjusted earnings of $1.70 a share versus $2.39, a violent reminder of how much operating leverage cuts both ways (fourth quarter of 2025).
- **Two-year top-line and earnings erosion** that the prose understates: revenue from $1.69B to $1.60B, earnings per share from $11.36 to $8.59, free cash flow down 20.5% year-over-year, a 43% beat rate.
- **Industrial's demand pillar is stimulus-dependent and admitted to be slowing** (that will slow... as we roll off some of this heavy infrastructure spend, third quarter of 2025); the snow business is declining by management's own quality-of-earnings choice (fourth quarter of 2025, first quarter of 2026).
- **Fresh macro caution** after the Iran war: rising freight and fuel costs, a slightly more cautious tone... over the last 30 days on agriculture (first quarter of 2026), right as the market-conditions notes flag an oil and inflation shock.

**Encouraging signs**
- **Industrial Equipment is a genuine compounder within the portfolio:** record sales, full-year-2025 profit margin of 17.7% (versus 15.7%), durable work-truck and municipal end-markets, share gains in snow and hydro-excavation (first quarter of 2025 through first quarter of 2026).
- **Fortress balance sheet:** net debt slashed from about $236M to about $11M through 2025, leverage below one year of profit even after funding the roughly $170M Petersen deal; about $195-310M cash; about $477M of available credit. Real special-situation optionality (every quarter).
- **Strong cash conversion:** free cash flow at 142% of net income in full-year 2025, 138% over the latest twelve months; earnings are cash, not accounting entries (fourth quarter of 2025, first quarter of 2026).
- **Dividend raised twice during the downturn** ($0.26 to $0.30 to $0.34, up 13.3% in February 2026), a confidence signal management was willing to put cash behind.
- **The first-quarter-2026 turn has evidence:** sequential adjusted earnings from $1.70 to $2.56, Vegetation profit margin from 2.3% to 11.2%, agricultural orders up double-digits in both the US and Europe; dealer inventories depleted... might be a little bit of a tailwind (first quarter of 2026).
- **The new CEO under-promising and over-delivering** versus a predecessor's over-promising, a stylistic credibility upgrade.

---

## 7. Hidden signals

- **The vocabulary shifted from growth to discipline and quality.** Leonard talked expansion, records, acquisitions breaking loose. Hureau talks quality of earnings (deliberately shrinking the low-margin snow business, fourth quarter of 2025, first quarter of 2026), procurement savings, footprint optimization, right-sizing. This is the language of a margin-repair operator, not a grower, telling about where the real opportunity (and problem) lies.
- **A new voice re-segmented the disclosure.** Under Hureau, reporting leans on adjusted profit and earnings with explicit add-backs (CEO transition, acquisition and integration, restructuring, inventory reserves), $3.2-3.5M of adjustments per quarter. More normalized-earnings framing; watch that the adjustments don't become recurring.
- **Capital allocation tilted decisively to small acquisitions.** Hureau named acquisitions a core pillar and is funding them with the pristine balance sheet (Petersen drew $120M from the credit line). The buyback Leonard mentioned in 2024 has gone quiet; cash is earmarked for deals and the (raised) dividend, not repurchases.
- **The divest-or-discontinue product lines tell (fourth quarter of 2025, first quarter of 2026).** Management is pruning Vegetation product lines (not brands) that do not fit where it is going and took inventory reserves against them. A subtle admission that parts of the legacy Vegetation portfolio are structurally impaired, and a small, undated self-help lever.
- **Hydro-excavation is the new organic-growth hook (third quarter of 2025):** required by OSHA and state rules, lightly penetrated, with a cited 6-7% annual growth rate. The one place management points to secular (not stimulus) Industrial growth; worth tracking as the genuine new driver.
- **The snow business was reframed from growth engine to margin-discipline story** within two quarters: we are not chasing that last dollar at low margins (fourth quarter of 2025). A real strategy change disguised as a sales miss.

---

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict (for the director)

**Classification: operationally turning, on a stable-to-improving base.** This is a fundamentally sound, cash-generating, near-net-cash industrial in the middle of a CEO-led margin-repair pivot, emerging from a cyclical Vegetation trough, with a genuinely strong Industrial franchise carrying it. It is not an event-driven special situation: there is no activist, no strategic review, no announced break-up or sale, and no transformational catalyst. The catalyst is an operating one: Vegetation end-market recovery plus the new CEO's roughly 300-basis-point (3-percentage-point) self-help margin program, both multi-year and both unconfirmed.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** is the first-quarter-2026 Vegetation turn (profit margin from 2.3% to 11.2%, agricultural orders up double-digits) the real bottom, or a seasonal head-fake into a softening macro? The entire re-rating to fair value depends on it. Two things argue against paying up now: first, the stock at about $149-153 sits above the Buffett-style and earnings-power fair value (a margin of safety, or discount to fair value, of about negative 6% to 7%), so the cheapness is relative to peers and dependent on discounted-cash-flow assumptions, not a hard discount to intrinsic worth; and second, management is itself flagging a more cautious tone on agriculture over the last 30 days, into the market-conditions notes' live oil and inflation shock that directly raises this company's steel, freight and chassis costs. The balance sheet gives downside protection and acquisition optionality; the missing piece is a dated, binding event to force the re-rating, and there isn't one. Size this as a quality-cyclical recovery position to accumulate on a Vegetation or macro flush, not as an asymmetric catalyst bet at a 52-week low that has already bounced.
